source_paper_title: Two Typical Implementable Semismooth* Newton Methods for Generalized Equations Are G-Semismooth Newton Methods
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Chen_2025, title={Two Typical Implementable Semismooth* Newton Methods for Generalized Equations Are G-Semismooth Newton Methods}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0617}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0617}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Chen, Liang and Sun, Defeng and Zhang, Wangyongquan}, year={2025}, month=jul }

Page 20, Section 6 (Conclusions).

Accordingly, a natural question is whether an implementable semismooth* Newton method is achievable for solving a GE that cannot be reformulated to locally Lipschitz continuous equations.
title: Design implementable semismooth Newton methods for generalized equations beyond Lipschitz reformulations
background_latex:
Let \(X,Y\) be finite-dimensional real Hilbert spaces. A generalized equation (GE) is an inclusion
\[
0\in \Phi(x),
\]
where \(\Phi: X\rightrightarrows Y\) is a set-valued mapping with closed graph.

A (single-valued) mapping \(H:U\to Y\) on an open set \(U\subseteq X\) is called locally Lipschitz continuous on \(U\) if there exists \(L>0\) such that \(\|H(x)-H(x')\|\le L\|x-x'\|\) for all \(x,x'\in U\). A GE is said to be reformulable (locally around a solution) as a locally Lipschitz continuous equation if there exists a neighborhood \(U\) of a reference solution \(\bar x\) and a locally Lipschitz mapping \(H:U\to Y\) such that, for \(x\in U\),
\[
0\in \Phi(x) \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad H(x)=0.
\]

Semismooth* Newton methods are Newton-type methods designed for set-valued inclusions \(0\in \Phi(x)\). In practical implementations discussed in this paper, a key ingredient is an explicitly computable \"approximation step\" that yields a Newton direction from solving a linear system.

The paper observes that two known implementable semismooth* Newton schemes (for two important GE classes) can be reinterpreted as G-semismooth Newton methods applied to a locally Lipschitz equation derived from the GE. This motivates asking whether similar implementable schemes exist when no such locally Lipschitz equation reformulation is available.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine conditions and a constructive procedure under which one can build an implementable semismooth* Newton-type method for a generalized equation \(0\in \Phi(x)\) with closed-graph set-valued mapping \(\Phi\), in the case where the GE cannot be reformulated (locally around a solution) as a locally Lipschitz continuous equation \(H(x)=0\).

In particular, design an algorithm that (as in known semismooth* Newton implementations) produces each Newton-type direction by solving a tractable linear system obtained from an explicit approximation step, while guaranteeing local Newton-type (e.g., superlinear) convergence under verifiable assumptions.
latex:
Let \(X,Y\) be finite-dimensional real Hilbert spaces. A generalized equation (GE) is an inclusion
\[
0\in \Phi(x),
\]
where \(\Phi: X\rightrightarrows Y\) is a set-valued mapping with closed graph.

A (single-valued) mapping \(H:U\to Y\) on an open set \(U\subseteq X\) is called locally Lipschitz continuous on \(U\) if there exists \(L>0\) such that \(\|H(x)-H(x')\|\le L\|x-x'\|\) for all \(x,x'\in U\). A GE is said to be reformulable (locally around a solution) as a locally Lipschitz continuous equation if there exists a neighborhood \(U\) of a reference solution \(\bar x\) and a locally Lipschitz mapping \(H:U\to Y\) such that, for \(x\in U\),
\[
0\in \Phi(x) \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad H(x)=0.
\]

Semismooth* Newton methods are Newton-type methods designed for set-valued inclusions \(0\in \Phi(x)\). In practical implementations discussed in this paper, a key ingredient is an explicitly computable \"approximation step\" that yields a Newton direction from solving a linear system.

The paper observes that two known implementable semismooth* Newton schemes (for two important GE classes) can be reinterpreted as G-semismooth Newton methods applied to a locally Lipschitz equation derived from the GE. This motivates asking whether similar implementable schemes exist when no such locally Lipschitz equation reformulation is available.

Determine conditions and a constructive procedure under which one can build an implementable semismooth* Newton-type method for a generalized equation \(0\in \Phi(x)\) with closed-graph set-valued mapping \(\Phi\), in the case where the GE cannot be reformulated (locally around a solution) as a locally Lipschitz continuous equation \(H(x)=0\).

In particular, design an algorithm that (as in known semismooth* Newton implementations) produces each Newton-type direction by solving a tractable linear system obtained from an explicit approximation step, while guaranteeing local Newton-type (e.g., superlinear) convergence under verifiable assumptions.
open_problem_quote: Accordingly, a natural question is whether an implementable semismooth* Newton method is achievable for solving a GE that cannot be reformulated to locally Lipschitz continuous equations.
subject_classification: Variational analysis and nonsmooth Newton methods for generalized equations
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for existence/design of a broad algorithmic framework rather than a crisp universally quantified statement with a finite-dimensional parameterization that could be falsified by searching instances. While one could search for pathological GEs, failure to find an implementable method on examples would not constitute a definitive counterexample to achievability in general.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: There is no single, programmatically checkable objective or benchmark specified (e.g., a fixed problem class with an evaluable performance metric). Without a concrete candidate representation (algorithm family) and an automatic convergence verifier across instances, an evolutionary coding loop lacks a reliable fitness signal.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
